sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 57, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ** - Board has a gray background with two white panels separated by a purple vertical bar - Left panel: dark gray box (with white interior) at lower-left, light green square with white center pixel - Between panels: light green square with black center pixel, white square - Right panel: dark gray box (with white interior) at upper area - Two green squares (same hash) appear to be agent-like objects with different inner colors (W vs B) - Goal likely involves moving green squares to interact with boxes or cross the purple barrier
- Plan: ** Try MOUSE to click on objects, or try moving green squares to box positions.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 56 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I see the bottom row (line 63) has a pattern: `ccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWW`
This looks like a progress bar or timer. The 'c' (charcoal) part and 'W' (white) part. This is likely the HUD.
Now let me check what LEFT did to the greens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what LEFT did
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        for child_id in n['children']:
            for cn in seg['nodes']:
                if cn['id'] == child_id:
                    print(f"Green: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, center={cn['color']}")

# Check the progress bar
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
progress = ascii_lines[63]
c_count = progress.count('c')
w_count = progress.count('W')
print(f"Progress bar: {c_count} c's, {w_count} W's")

# Compare to previous frame's progress bar
if previous_frame:
    prev_lines = previous_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    prev_progress = prev_lines[63]
    prev_c = prev_progress.count('c')
    prev_w = prev_progress.count('W')
    print(f"Previous progress: {prev_c} c's, {prev_w} W's")
    print(f"Progress changed: {progress != prev_progress}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see! LEFT moved the LEFT green from cols 30-32 to cols 27-29 (back to its initial position!). The right green stayed at cols 45-47.
So LEFT only moves the left green, and RIGHT only moves the right green? No wait, earlier when I pressed RIGHT, only 1 cell changed (the progress bar), and the greens didn't move. But now LEFT moved the left green.
Wait, let me reconsider. Earlier, RIGHT was executed but the ASCII didn't change (except for the progress bar). But LEFT just moved the left green. So maybe RIGHT doesn't move the right green when it's already at the rightmost position?
Actually, looking at the positions:
- Left green: was at cols 30-32, LEFT moved it to cols 27-29
- Right green: still at cols 45-47
So LEFT moves the left green leftward. But earlier, RIGHT didn't move the right green. Maybe RIGHT only moves the left green rightward?
Wait, I need to reconsider. Let me check: in the initial state, left green was at cols 18-20 and right green at cols 27-29. Now left green is at cols 27-29 and right green at cols 45-47. So:
- Left green moved from 18-20 to 27-29 (9 steps right)
- Right green moved from 27-29 to 45-47 (18 steps right)
But LEFT/RIGHT should move both greens the same amount if they move together. Unless they move independently.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe:
- LEFT moves the left green leftward
- RIGHT moves the right green rightward
- UP/DOWN moves the right green up/down
This would explain why the right green is at cols 45-47 (moved right many times) and the left green is at cols 27-29 (moved right some times, then left once).
But wait, earlier when I pressed RIGHT, the green didn't move (only progress bar changed). Maybe RIGHT has a limit - the right green can't move further right?
Actually, looking at the board, the right panel ends around col 53. The right green at cols 45-47 could still move right. But maybe there's a wall or boundary.
Let me try RIGHT again and see if it moves the right green now.
Actually, I realize I should focus on the goal. The progress bar changed from 29 c's to 28 c's (and 35 W's to 36 W's). So each action increments the progress bar. This is likely a step counter or timer, not the goal itself.
The goal is probably to get the greens into the boxes with the correct center colors.
Let me think about what the "correct" configuration is:
- Left box: rows 32-36, cols 11-15, interior is white
- Right box: rows 26-30, cols 44-48, interior is white
- Left green: rows 30-32, cols 27-29, center=W
- Right green: rows 30-32, cols 45-47, center=c
For the left green to be in the left box:
- Rows: 30-32 vs box 32-36. The green needs to move DOWN to rows 33-35 (box interior).
- Cols: 27-29 vs box 11-15. The green needs to move LEFT to cols 12-14 (box interior).
But the left green can only move LEFT/RIGHT, not UP/DOWN. So it can get to the right column range but not the right row range.
Unless... the left green doesn't need to go into the left box. Maybe the goal is different.
For the right green to be in the right box:
- Rows: 30-32 vs box 26-30. The green needs to move UP to rows 27-29 (box interior).
- Cols: 45-47 vs box 44-48. The green is already in the right column range!
So the right green just needs to move UP. But UP has been failing.
Wait, earlier UP did work when the right green was at rows 30-32, cols 45-47. It moved to rows 27-29. But now UP doesn't work. What's different?
Maybe the center color matters. The right green center is now 'c'. Maybe UP only works when the center is a specific color.
Let me check: initially the right green center was B. When did it become c?
Looking at my history, the right green center was B initially. At some point it became c. I think it changed when I clicked it or when it moved.
Let me try clicking the right green to toggle its center, then try UP.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Right green at rows 30-32, cols 45-47, center=c
# Try clicking to toggle center, then UP
# Click center of right green: row 31, col 46
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 46}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
